items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Skellig Michael
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* kingdom of munster
*County:* County Kerry
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 6th century
*Coords:* 51.77111111, -10.54055556
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skellig_Michael
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Skellig%20Michael/@51.7707,-10.5395,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Skellig_Michael03%28js%29.jpg/330px-Skellig_Michael03%28js%29.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://heritageireland.ie/places-to-visit/sceilg-mhichil/

*Description:*
Skellig Michael, also called Great Skellig, is a twin-pinnacled crag 11.6 kilometres (7.2 mi) west of the Iveragh Peninsula in County Kerry, Ireland. The island is named after the archangel Michael; while "Skellig" is derived from the Irish language word sceilig, meaning a splinter of stone. Its twin island, Little Skellig, is smaller and inaccessible. The two islands were formed c. 374–360 million years ago during a period of mountain formation, along with the local MacGillycuddy's Reeks mountain range. They were later separated from the mainland by rising water levels.

_Listing 1 of 10_

listing.name: Skellig Michael
listing.desc: Skellig Michael, also called Great Skellig, is a twin-pinnacled crag 11.6 kilometres (7.2 mi) west of the Iveragh Peninsula in County Kerry, Ireland. The island is named after the archangel Michael; while "Skellig" is derived from the Irish language word sceilig, meaning a splinter of stone. Its twin island, Little Skellig, is smaller and inaccessible. The two islands were formed c. 374–360 million years ago during a period of mountain formation, along with the local MacGillycuddy's Reeks mountain range. They were later separated from the mainland by rising water levels.

*Name:* Newgrange Cursus
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* irelands ancient east
*County:* County Meath
*Type:* fort
*Period:* c. 3100 BC
*Coords:* 53.695135, -6.473093
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newgrange_cursus
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Newgrange%20Cursus/@53.694,-6.476,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/newgrange-cursus.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* no
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/AaZtVSpKoMd9GgC99

*Description:*
The Newgrange cursus is a Neolithic monument used as a ceremonial procession route within the Brú na Bóinne complex. The ancient trackway is 100m long and 20m wide. It is located at Newgrange, in County Meath, Ireland.

_Listing 2 of 10_

listing.desc: The Newgrange cursus is a Neolithic monument used as a ceremonial procession route within the Brú na Bóinne complex. The ancient trackway is 100m long and 20m wide. It is located at Newgrange, in County Meath, Ireland.

*Name:* Housesteads Roman Fort
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Northumberland
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 2nd century
*Coords:* 55.013, -2.331
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Housesteads_Roman_Fort
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Housesteads%20Roman%20Fort/@55.0117,-2.3283,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMNR60AqwnQLZ4im9lTeM2Wp33yvkRHJxeaaU5bKX1jcoZ65iomnlOGgGdFDUMxWBiOSoQitM-NaG-dvtSur_U2wddB4vn7-YGwkIo0BkRV91QwfqrBIH8L2a1Fp179OBLtDmiBYW1DyPp4=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Housesteads Roman Fort was an auxiliary fort on Hadrian's Wall, at Housesteads, Northumberland, England. It is dramatically positioned on the end of the 1-mile (1.6 km)-long crag of the Whin Sill over which the Wall runs, overlooking sparsely populated hills.

_Listing 1 of 10_

listing.desc: Housesteads Roman Fort was an auxiliary fort on Hadrian's Wall, at Housesteads, Northumberland, England. It is dramatically positioned on the end of the 1-mile (1.6 km)-long crag of the Whin Sill over which the Wall runs, overlooking sparsely populated hills.

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Kilpeck Church
*Country:* england
*Region:* the midlands
*County:* Herefordshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 51.9703, -2.8092
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Church_of_St_Mary_and_St_David,_Kilpeck
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Kilpeck%20Church/@51.9567,-2.8883,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/KilpeckChurch%28PhilipHalling%29Feb2006.jpg/330px-KilpeckChurch%28PhilipHalling%29Feb2006.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
The Church of St Mary and St David is a Church of England parish church at Kilpeck in the English county of Herefordshire, about 5 miles from the border with Monmouthshire, Wales. Pevsner describes Kilpeck as "one of the most perfect Norman churches in England". Famous for its stone carvings, the church is a Grade I listed building.

_Listing 2 of 10_

listing.name: Kilpeck Church
listing.desc: The Church of St Mary and St David is a Church of England parish church at Kilpeck in the English county of Herefordshire, about 5 miles from the border with Monmouthshire, Wales. Pevsner describes Kilpeck as "one of the most perfect Norman churches in England". Famous for its stone carvings, the church is a Grade I listed building.

imageAttribution.description: St Mary's & St David's parish church, Kilpeck, Herefordshire: view from the southeast, showing the apsidal chancel
